text:
Scale-out CPO switches going from 5,000 units this year to 200,000 by 2030. And you are selling?

That’s a 144% CAGR. A 40x volume ramp that both Yole and Morgan Stanley are modeling off the same wall. That’s the whole thesis, and notice what it doesn’t require: it doesn’t require me to pick which laser vendor qualifies into which switch, or whether Broadcom’s socket beats Nvidia’s.

To be short this ramp up you have to argue the entire build doesn’t happen, and good luck making that case while the SemiAnalysis “delay” everyone panicked over still lands you at 200k units by 2030. 

A delay bends the curve doesn’t make it go away. 

The physics won’t let it go away. A 200,000 GPU cluster is burning ~17 megawatts just on transceivers. CPO cuts that ~73% per 800G. At scale-up density copper is already dead and pluggables are out of runway. Forced migrations are the best ramps you can be long because the demand doesn’t care.

The biggest winners:

Lasers and light sources: $LITE, $COHR, $SIVE
Optical engines and pump lasers: $AAOI, $COHR
Switch ASICs: $AVGO, $MRVL, $NVDA
Optical I/O chiplets: $ALAB, $CRDO
Packaging and assembly: $FN, $AMKR
InP substrate and materials: $AXTI

LITE is the cleanest CPO scarcity play, shipping 200G-per-lane EMLs at volume, book through 2028. $AAOI is the one that wins both ways: its core 800G and 1.6T pluggable business actually benefits if CPO slips, while its external laser fab keeps it in the game when CPO finally ramps.

Bullish photonics ⚡️

text:
2026/07/08
US market regime: 74/100
Strong, but slightly overheated. Reversal risk is rising.

Trend is flat. Bear alerts: 2/11.
The market is still strong, but momentum is slowing from recent highs. Major indices remain near the upper end of their 52-week range, while internal signals are getting mixed.

✅ Bullish factors:
Both cap-weighted and equal-weighted measures are moving higher
65% of stocks are above their 200DMA
Dollar-volume participation is 1.31x, now $882B, +9% vs 20 days ago and +40% over 3 months
Credit remains healthy
VIX term structure is calm
S&P 500 has a follow-through day signal

⚠️ Risk factors:
SPY has 7 distribution days
QQQ has 6 distribution days
Accumulation/distribution is 0.82, leaning toward distribution
Sentiment is 72, in greed territory
Leveraged ETF enthusiasm is elevated, with bull ratio at 77%
Offense/defense rotation is -1.1σ, slightly defensive
Nasdaq weakness is still visible

【Internals】
🔸Breadth: 65% above 200DMA = still healthy
🔸Participation: 1.31x / $882B = liquidity is expanding
🔸Accumulation/distribution: 0.82 = distribution bias. This is the main internal concern
🔸Leader strength: 68% = strong, but approaching overheated territory
🔸McClellan/RANA: +3.5 = neutral
🔸Offense/defense rotation: -1.1σ = slightly defensive
🔸Sentiment: 72 = greed

【Risk metrics】
Expected 1-month move, 1σ:
SPY ±4.7%, downside reference 713
QQQ ±8.1%, downside reference 652

Distribution days:
SPY 7 = correction warning
QQQ 6 = watch / caution

Sentiment:
Leveraged ETF heat is 93.
Parabolic stock ratio is 51.
SKEW is 146.

This is overheated, but not an automatic sell signal. Overheating is normal during strong uptrends.

【Capital rotation】
Capital is centered in:
semiconductors, manufacturing supply chains, and energy

Continued inflows:
payment networks, healthcare IT, fintech SaaS

Continued outflows:
lithium mining, AI optical/photonics, cable/satellite equipment

Key takeaway:
Participation and breadth remain strong. The liquidity inflow is real.
But accumulation/distribution is at 0.82, SPY and QQQ distribution days are piling up, and sentiment is getting hot.

So this is not a “buy everything because the market is strong” setup.
It is a selective market.

I’m focusing only on strong themes where pullback, volume, and relative strength line up.
No blind chasing here.

Not financial advice.

text:
Semiconductor & photonics names across the list took a broad hit today, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index shedding over 5% following a classic "sell-the-news" reaction to Samsung's record Q2 earnings — a 19-fold surge in operating profit that still fell short of elevated expectations, triggering aggressive profit-taking across AI-linked chip and optical stocks. Morgan Stanley's call to reduce semiconductor exposure and DeepSeek's reported development of an in-house AI inference chip added further headwinds to the sector.

AXT Inc.  was among the hardest hit, dropping -8.5% to $58.15 on elevated volume of over 8.3M shares. The pullback follows Monday's +19% two-day surge driven by a landmark $22.3M prepaid supply agreement with Coherent — that deal's fundamentals remain intact, and Northland Capital holds an Outperform rating with a $125 price target, but today's move reflects broader sector rotation rather than company-specific deterioration. Notably, AXTI still trades well off its 52-week high of $143.16, underscoring the volatility inherent in this high-beta AI wafer play.
Coherent Corp.  shed -6.4% to $314.13, giving back recent gains despite a strong fundamental backdrop — FQ3 revenue of $1.81B (+21% YoY), a $2B NVIDIA investment, and a Wall Street median price target of $372.50 all suggest the selloff is valuation-driven rather than thesis-breaking. Applied Optoelectronics  fell -7.3% to $114.41, compounded by mixed analyst sentiment (consensus Hold) and notable insider selling of over 500,000 shares in the past quarter. Sive  and 0ND2.L  also declined sharply at -11.7% and -12.7% respectively, consistent with the broader risk-off tone hitting smaller-cap names hardest.

The overarching theme is a multi-day whipsaw in AI hardware and semiconductor supply chain stocks — last week's selloff, Monday's partial rebound, and today's renewed pressure illustrate how sensitive these names remain to macro sentiment shifts around AI capex sustainability.

text:
Fascinating breakthrough from Osaka Metropolitan University on this phase-change magneto-optical metagrating device! Steering heat non-reciprocally at near-straight-on angles, separating absorption/emission, switching with GST, storing states power-free, and tuning via magnetic fields is huge for thermal photonics.
Here’s a speculative systems-level concept building directly on it for future high-performance 3D chip stacks (AI accelerators, HPC, etc.):
Hierarchical “Thermo-Magnetic Intelligent Packaging” Architecture
Chip-level (micro): Integrate small metagratings on the backside or edges of compute dies. Waste heat naturally drives resonant mid-IR (~13μm) emission. GST enables on/off latching of nonreciprocity; magnetic field provides continuous spectral/frequency tuning and steering. Modulated incoherent IR carries:
•  Operational control logic
•  Thermal status/hotspot data
•  Commands for dynamic management
•  Low-speed sideband/backup memory (non-urgent data, configs, logs, checkpoints)
This keeps the main silicon cores 100% dedicated to high-speed data processing—no control overhead.
Macro control level (package/board): A distributed network of Ni/Ti (Nitinol) elements handles global orchestration:
•  Automated feedback loops for thermal balancing across the entire stack.
•  Excess heat removal via actuated/curved paths through insulating seals to sinks.
•  Structural support and mechanical stability for the 3D assembly.
•  Field producers: Ni/Ti wires/structures generate or modulate the required transverse magnetic fields (~1T regionally) to steer the chip-level IR emission without placing magnets directly on delicate silicon.
•  Multi-tasking sensors (temperature via resistance/shape response) + electrical switching.
How it works in operation:
•  Chips generate heat → metagratings modulate and directionally steer IR (short-range only: mm to cm, as far as needed for local heat removal).
•  IR signal travels to macro detectors or adjacent layers, carrying control data.
•  Macro Ni/Ti network senses globally, actuates heat routing, adjusts magnetic fields for optimal steering, and balances load/heat across the stack in real time.
•  Closed-loop, low-power (non-volatile GST + shape-memory states), self-aware thermal management.
Benefits:
•  Turns waste heat into a useful control + sideband communication medium.
•  Massive parallelism (many extraction paths per chip/layer) boosts effective bandwidth for control traffic.
•  Clean separation: Compute optimized for speed; thermal/control offloaded to the “heat fabric.”
•  Improved resilience, predictive maintenance, and efficiency in dense 3D stacks.
•  Scalable with existing advanced packaging (interposers, hybrid bonding).
This isn’t a near-term product—just an extrapolation showing how your device’s nonreciprocal heat steering + non-volatile switching could enable truly intelligent, heat-utilizing systems. No extra laser heat bleed; everything rides on waste heat management.
What do you think—any other applications for programmable thermal radiation like this in sensors, energy harvesting, or photonics? Great work by the team! @OsakaMetUniv_en @physorg_com

text: Not many people were active pre-Asia crisis. In July of ‘97 I was sent by my employer to live in Singapore for 2 months to help take down the Baht - I was 29. We spent the next several years buying cheap assets and selling them for incredible returns into the 00s. I don’t think people can comprehend the leverage and gambling mentality inherent in Asian bubbles.. I’m not even sure this is a bubble yet. But, the speculative money/structure unwind feels pretty orderly in Korea.  Obviously 40% sell-offs in single names - and there are many - doesn’t feel orderly, but this is not what one would call contagion. Iran aside, I have no clue if the QQQs will rally or the Kospi will rally from here/tonight, but I look at what’s been thrown at this market for the past few years and look where we are in the indexes & I don’t see fear. With all of this said, I don’t think fear is warranted at the moment.. and, like most, I have spent way too much time and effort and $$ over the past 6months trying to understand finite details of the Ai build out, bottlenecks, substrates, lasers, photonics, etc.  If anything, I think we have just ended that period of mental twister and for that, I am grateful.

text:
Everyone's thinking the same thing here: "what's happening to AI stocks?"

So, I'll explain what I think is happening.

And where I see us moving forward.

Let's deep dive:

I think most of these are going through a "distribution" formation.

Check the $SIVE chart below for what this means.

But basically, we have a sell off here, until we start accumulating (or showing signs of higher highs, and a it being a good spot people bid at).

Now we do have some fundamental reasons for this: 

Firstly, SemiAnalysis wrote a piece on CPO delays and company valuations. 

This caused the first sell off. 

$NVDA and other optical companies refuted these claims, though.

Secondly, we had a "sell the news" event on earnings and expections. 

$MU and Samsung's recent earning results were very strong, but it seems to not have been the massive beating investors were hoping for. 

So, this triggered sharp drops in memory and chip stocks ($MU, $SNDK, etc.), spilling over to the broader sector. Investors had priced in perfection.

I mean, check the past chart growth when this is really priced in for future growth % potential. 

Thirdly, we've had some "is this a bubble" fears.

AI infrastructure spending is enormous (hyperscalers like $MSFT , $GOOGL, $META, $AMZN on track for hundreds of billions), but questions linger on the short-term ROI. Is this sustainable?

Especially with the $META renting out $NBIS compute. 

People think it shows $META might not need as much compute in the future. So we saw a mass sell off on some names: $NBIS, $BRUN, $IREN, $HIVE, etc., but I don't think this will last.

I wrote about this earlier (just look at my earlier posts on $NBIS).

And enablers (such as $NVDA, $AMD, $AVGO, $MU, photonics plays like $SIVE, optical interconnects, power/cooling) ran up hugely on the buildout narrative. Profit-taking + rotation out of "expensive" tech is natural after crazy pumps (natural human psychology imo).

Fourth, we got some macro pressures holding us down (not limited to these companies, but they hurt the risk-on ones most): 

Interest rates/inflation: Fears of higher rates (Fed outlook, oil prices from geopolitics like Iran-related tensions) raise borrowing costs for the capital-intensive AI buildout. Debt financing for data centers and infra is surging too, so that doesn't help.

Geopolitical noise and strong jobs data have added volatility as well.

And lastly, as I'm writing this, news just broke out that the U.S. has started launching strikes against Iran again... so that's an added confluence we needed lol.

But, soon, I think we'll getting into the classic accumulation zone.

I think the $BTC -> AI pivot companies will hold best here, as crypto has bottomed imo.

But for the rest, I think we have a bit lower to go. Maybe just a tad.

In particular, $NBIS has a clear zone at 170 that I'm looking at for support. I'm hoping it holds there and accumulates for a bit before sending higher.

For $OPTX, I think this is in a consolidation phase, and $ASYS as well.

This really applies to all of these stocks (even $AAOI, $CRWV, and any other one you can think of).

We accumulate here before sending higher.

It's not going to be the "V" shape you all know and love.

But with due time, we will go up again imo.

So, I'll explain what I think is happening. And where I see us moving forward. Let's deep dive: I think most of these are going through a "distribution" formation. Check the $SIVE chart below for what this means. But basically, we have a sell off here, until we start accumulating (or showing signs of higher highs, and a it being a good spot people bid at). Now we do have some fundamental reasons for this: Firstly, SemiAnalysis wrote a piece on CPO delays and company valuations. This caused the first sell off. $NVDA and other optical companies refuted these claims, though. Secondly, we had a "sell the news" event on earnings and expections. $MU and Samsung's recent earning results were very strong, but it seems to not have been the massive beating investors were hoping for. So, this triggered sharp drops in memory and chip stocks ($MU, $SNDK, etc.), spilling over to the broader sector. Investors had priced in perfection. I mean, check the past chart growth when this is really priced in for future growth % potential. Thirdly, we've had some "is this a bubble" fears. AI infrastructure spending is enormous (hyperscalers like $MSFT , $GOOGL, $META, $AMZN on track for hundreds of billions), but questions linger on the short-term ROI. Is this sustainable? Especially with the $META renting out $NBIS compute. People think it shows $META might not need as much compute in the future. So we saw a mass sell off on some names: $NBIS, $BRUN, $IREN, $HIVE, etc., but I don't think this will last. I wrote about this earlier (just look at my earlier posts on $NBIS). And enablers (such as $NVDA, $AMD, $AVGO, $MU, photonics plays like $SIVE, optical interconnects, power/cooling) ran up hugely on the buildout narrative. Profit-taking + rotation out of "expensive" tech is natural after crazy pumps (natural human psychology imo). Fourth, we got some macro pressures holding us down (not limited to these companies, but they hurt the risk-on ones most): Interest rates/inflation: Fears of higher rates (Fed outlook, oil prices from geopolitics like Iran-related tensions) raise borrowing costs for the capital-intensive AI buildout. Debt financing for data centers and infra is surging too, so that doesn't help. Geopolitical noise and strong jobs data have added volatility as well. And lastly, as I'm writing this, news just broke out that the U.S. has started launching strikes against Iran again... so that's an added confluence we needed lol. But, soon, I think we'll getting into the classic accumulation zone. I think the $BTC -> AI pivot companies will hold best here, as crypto has bottomed imo. But for the rest, I think we have a bit lower to go. Maybe just a tad. In particular, $NBIS has a clear zone at 170 that I'm looking at for support. I'm hoping it holds there and accumulates for a bit before sending higher. For $OPTX, I think this is in a consolidation phase, and $ASYS as well. This really applies to all of these stocks (even $AAOI, $CRWV, and any other one you can think of). We accumulate here before sending higher. It's not going to be the "V" shape you all know and love. But with due time, we will go up again imo."}}}, "rest_id": "2074610847473668106", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10891", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Lol… Non exclusive only matters when there is a second supplier.

Who exactly are they qualifying?

$GFS can qualify a second supplier whenever they want. But there is no known alternative in their qualification pipeline right now.

InP DFB lasers at this spec and volume is one of the hardest things to manufacture in photonics.

Non exclusive only matters if a credible replacement exists.

text:
While AAOI’s primary business is building traditional pluggable optical transceivers, they also develop foundational high-power lasers and external laser sources for CPO architectures. So they’re bridging the gap today while also prepping for what comes next. CPO is a 2028/29 story at best. But the point is having a fundamental basis to justify a stock price today. Whether on a visible discounted cash flow basis or just material revenue/backlog growth TODAY. AAOI has explosive revenue growth actually materializing, on top of a surging **backlog**, whereas Sivers is baiting retail with a merely subjective **opportunity pipeline**, with zero photonics revenue today, let alone even a backlog to show for anything. Just non-exclusive partnerships on reference designs that they hope will be used by 2028. And pricing power will be fully at the discretion of WIN Semi, who they outsource designs to, further eroding potential margins should anything even manifest. I mean hey, this thing could surely pump again, but the risks are IMMENSE and it will only run on hype, which is intrinsically fizzling out (especially with all this dilution and some serious macro risks on the horizon/liquidity tightening). I suspect some funky business going on with their management, and I’m merely trying to make people aware not to blindly follow anonymous pump pages

Scaling silicon photonics from the lab to high-volume manufacturing requires more than precision. It demands automation, repeatability, and throughput.

FormFactor TRITON™,  the industry's first fully integrated silicon photonics test cell, combines optical and electrical wafer test, nano-precision alignment, and factory automation to help manufacturers scale production with confidence.

text:
Paradis Labs ยังมั่นใจ $AAOI และ $AEHR แม้หุ้น AI Small Cap ปรับฐานแรง

Paradis Labs ระบุว่ายังคงถือหุ้น AAOI และ AEHR พร้อมแสดงความเชื่อมั่นต่อแนวโน้มระยะยาวของทั้งสองบริษัท แม้ว่าหุ้นกลุ่ม AI Infrastructure ขนาดเล็กจะถูกแรงขายกดดันในช่วงที่ผ่านมา

สำหรับ AAOI มองว่า

ตั้งเป้ารายได้จาก Optical Transceiver แตะประมาณ 471 ล้านดอลลาร์ต่อเดือนภายในกลางปี 2027
บริษัทกำลังขยายกำลังการผลิตรองรับผลิตภัณฑ์ 800G และ 1.6T
แม้เพิ่มกำลังผลิตแล้ว คาดว่าอุปสงค์จะยังสูงกว่ากำลังการผลิตจนถึงประมาณไตรมาส 3 ปี 2027

ส่วน AEHR

เพิ่งได้รับคำสั่งซื้อเพิ่มเติมสำหรับระบบ Wafer Level Burn-In (WLBI) จากลูกค้า Silicon Photonics
มีโอกาสได้รับคำสั่งซื้อเพิ่มภายในปีนี้ หลังผ่านการรับรองเป็นซัพพลายเออร์แล้ว
ก่อนหน้านี้ยังได้รับคำสั่งซื้อระบบ Package Level Burn-In (PLBI) จากลูกค้า Hyperscaler รายใหญ่

Paradis Labs ระบุว่า หุ้นทั้งสองตัวมีความผันผวนสูง (High Beta) จึงถูกแรงขายในรอบ AI Selloff มากกว่าหุ้นขนาดใหญ่ แต่ยังเชื่อมั่นในแนวโน้มระยะยาวและยังคงถือการลงทุนต่อ

And still hold my positions.

With $AAOI:
> Target ~$471M monthly transceiver revenue mid-2027.
> Capacity ramp is happening now for 800G/1.6T.
> Even with capacity expansion, demand is expected to exceed production capacity until ~Q3

text:
Be warned!! This is a little long but VERY informative on who Kopin should be prospecting and timeline of execution.

I KNOW we are early on $KOPN with its new MicroLED Neural I/o chip design. And its DEMO to be presented end of 2026. So trying to take advantage while their is time, #KOPIN should be looking into hyperscalers first. 

Hyperscalers & Cloud AI Builders (Top Priority)

NVIDIA (GPU clusters, NVLink complements), Google (TPUs), Amazon (Trainium/Inferentia), Microsoft (Azure Maia/custom silicon), Meta, Oracle, xAI, and others building massive AI training/inference clusters.

WHY? 
They face acute power and bandwidth walls in dense GPU racks. Power/cooling is a top TCO driver; Neural I/o promises major efficiency gains for intra-rack or short inter-rack GPU-to-GPU and GPU-to-memory traffic.
AI Chip/Accelerator Designers
NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom (custom ASICs), and startups (Cerebras, Groq, SambaNova, etc.). Also advanced packaging players (TSMC, Intel, Samsung) for chiplet or co-packaged optics (CPO) integration.

WHY? 
Direct integration into next-gen accelerators or 2.5D/3D packages. Supports high-parallelism needs without heavy SerDes bottlenecks.

Server/System OEMs & Integrators Supermicro, Dell, HPE, Lenovo.

WHY? 
They build the racks/servers sold to hyperscalers. Better interconnects enable higher-density, lower-power systems that differentiate their offerings. Networking & Photonics Ecosystem Broadcom (AVGO—networking ASICs/optics leader), Arista, Cisco, plus photonics firms (Lumentum/LITE, Coherent). Potential partners or complementors rather than pure competitors for specific short-reach use cases.

Defense, Government & Sovereign AI Infrastructure

DoD/intelligence community data centers, contractors, and sovereign AI projects (Europe, Middle East, Asia) seeking secure/domestic supply chains.

WHY?
Kopin’s US-based full MicroLED production capability + long defense heritage (displays, soldier systems) provides a strong moat for secure AI hardware. Dual-use potential with existing defense MicroLED work.

Advantages of $KOPN vs. Current Data Center Interconnects

Neural I/o sits in a “sweet spot” between copper’s limitations and traditional laser optics’ drawbacks for short-to-medium reach, high-bandwidth AI intra-cluster links. It draws from “wide-and-slow” parallel channel concepts (hundreds/thousands of lower-speed MicroLED channels instead of a few ultra-high-speed lanes), similar to Microsoft Research’s MOSAIC project (which demonstrated ~56-68% lower power, up to 50m reach, and much higher reliability using MicroLEDs + imaging fiber).Neural I/o wins on bandwidth scalability (parallelism enables 1.6 Tbps demo target soon, 3.6 Tbps roadmap), dramatically lower power per bit, less heat (easier cooling, higher rack density), and better reach for board/rack scales. It “breaks the copper wall” while keeping costs and power closer to copper-like economics for these reaches.Vs. Traditional Optical (InP/VCSEL lasers, silicon photonics transceivers, pluggable optics or CPO):
Lasers excel at longer reach but are power-hungry, expensive, thermally sensitive, complex (heavy DSP, CDR, FEC, equalization, SerDes), and overkill (plus higher failure rates) for many short intra-rack or chip-to-chip links in AI clusters. They add cost, power, and complexity where not fully needed.

Neural I/o wins by using MicroLEDs as direct emitters (cheaper, lower power, more temperature-robust, simpler drive electronics possible with parallel low-speed channels). It eliminates or reduces need for expensive laser modules and complex digital backends for targeted use cases. Potential for smaller form factor, higher reliability/redundancy (pixel-level), and better power efficiency (comparable to MOSAIC’s ~3-5W vs. 10-12W per link range in research). Bidirectional capability from NeuralDisplay architecture is a plus. It avoids “expensive laser-based systems entirely” for these applications while delivering needed bandwidth.

LOOONG $KOPN

And its DEMO to be presented end of 2026. So trying to take advantage while their is time, #KOPIN should be looking into hyperscalers first. Hyperscalers & Cloud AI Builders (Top Priority) NVIDIA (GPU clusters, NVLink complements), Google (TPUs), Amazon (Trainium/Inferentia), Microsoft (Azure Maia/custom silicon), Meta, Oracle, xAI, and others building massive AI training/inference clusters. WHY? They face acute power and bandwidth walls in dense GPU racks. Power/cooling is a top TCO driver; Neural I/o promises major efficiency gains for intra-rack or short inter-rack GPU-to-GPU and GPU-to-memory traffic. AI Chip/Accelerator Designers NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom (custom ASICs), and startups (Cerebras, Groq, SambaNova, etc.). Also advanced packaging players (TSMC, Intel, Samsung) for chiplet or co-packaged optics (CPO) integration. WHY? Direct integration into next-gen accelerators or 2.5D/3D packages. Supports high-parallelism needs without heavy SerDes bottlenecks. Server/System OEMs & Integrators Supermicro, Dell, HPE, Lenovo. WHY? They build the racks/servers sold to hyperscalers. Better interconnects enable higher-density, lower-power systems that differentiate their offerings. Networking & Photonics Ecosystem Broadcom (AVGO—networking ASICs/optics leader), Arista, Cisco, plus photonics firms (Lumentum/LITE, Coherent). Potential partners or complementors rather than pure competitors for specific short-reach use cases. Defense, Government & Sovereign AI Infrastructure DoD/intelligence community data centers, contractors, and sovereign AI projects (Europe, Middle East, Asia) seeking secure/domestic supply chains. WHY? Kopin’s US-based full MicroLED production capability + long defense heritage (displays, soldier systems) provides a strong moat for secure AI hardware. Dual-use potential with existing defense MicroLED work. Advantages of $KOPN vs. Current Data Center Interconnects Neural I/o sits in a “sweet spot” between copper’s limitations and traditional laser optics’ drawbacks for short-to-medium reach, high-bandwidth AI intra-cluster links. It draws from “wide-and-slow” parallel channel concepts (hundreds/thousands of lower-speed MicroLED channels instead of a few ultra-high-speed lanes), similar to Microsoft Research’s MOSAIC project (which demonstrated ~56-68% lower power, up to 50m reach, and much higher reliability using MicroLEDs + imaging fiber).Neural I/o wins on bandwidth scalability (parallelism enables 1.6 Tbps demo target soon, 3.6 Tbps roadmap), dramatically lower power per bit, less heat (easier cooling, higher rack density), and better reach for board/rack scales. It “breaks the copper wall” while keeping costs and power closer to copper-like economics for these reaches.Vs. Traditional Optical (InP/VCSEL lasers, silicon photonics transceivers, pluggable optics or CPO): Lasers excel at longer reach but are power-hungry, expensive, thermally sensitive, complex (heavy DSP, CDR, FEC, equalization, SerDes), and overkill (plus higher failure rates) for many short intra-rack or chip-to-chip links in AI clusters. They add cost, power, and complexity where not fully needed. Neural I/o wins by using MicroLEDs as direct emitters (cheaper, lower power, more temperature-robust, simpler drive electronics possible with parallel low-speed channels). It eliminates or reduces need for expensive laser modules and complex digital backends for targeted use cases. Potential for smaller form factor, higher reliability/redundancy (pixel-level), and better power efficiency (comparable to MOSAIC’s ~3-5W vs. 10-12W per link range in research). Bidirectional capability from NeuralDisplay architecture is a plus. It avoids “expensive laser-based systems entirely” for these applications while delivering needed bandwidth. text:
Daily Buzzberg Alpha

the tape isn't sending one macro signal. it's repricing a regime: geopolitical supply risk returning while ai capex becomes an earnings tailwind AND an inflation/rates risk at the same time.

that combination kills generic beta and creates dispersion. the market has stopped paying for ai exposure or risk-on exposure — it's paying for contracted demand, validated supply-chain position, and balance-sheet capacity. it's punishing crowded trades where the thesis is intact but the holder base is impaired.

the whole note reduces to one filter: contracts > stories.

— where the asymmetry sits —

$WYFI — cleanest setup in the neocloud wreckage. ~$1b+ contracted backlog vs ~$1.3b market cap, and nscale's $900m credit facility validates the counterparty. sold with the basket, priced like the contracts don't exist.

$AVGO — the one ai print this week backed by a signed deal: apple's $30b+ agreement + $1.5b colorado buildout. validates the custom asic layer beyond gpu scarcity. revenue visibility, not vibes — and the cleaner read-through than $AAPL, which carries higher domestic cost and eu overhang.

$ONDS — thesis tied to numbers, not narrative: path to ~$1b revenue by 2027, net-income inflection, sum-of-parts floor, q2 print as the catalyst.

photonics — the under-the-surface one. inp substrates easing from "very tight" to "tight" isn't bearish — it lets downstream optical names convert interconnect demand into shipments. distinguish upstream bottleneck vs downstream qualification moats. $LITE $AAOI $COHR

$RKLB — flow unusually specific: 200dma entry, ms overweight $105 pt, vertical-integration bull case. blue origin's $130b private raise now anchors every public space comp.

— crowded, entry decayed —

$PENG — textbook signal decay. real discovery a month ago (earnings beat, cxl exposure, nvidia ai factory partner). now the flow is victory laps and partial trims. thesis quality high, entry quality gone.

broad ai beta — warsh's divided fomc tied ai capex to upside inflation risk, some members already floating hikes. that's the mechanism: the buildout that feeds suppliers also keeps real rates sticky, so long-duration ai without contract visibility underperforms. $TLT stays exposed.

— battleground —

memory — highest-dispersion area on the tape. korean flow reads the samsung/hynix selloff as forced deleveraging (levered-etf + retail-credit mechanics, adr re-rating catalyst) with hbm4/rubin demand structural through 2028. us flow is chaos — "memory is dead" next to aggressive $MU $SNDK dip buys. right framing: thesis intact, ownership base damaged. tradable, not clean.

oil — the move isn't the crude print, it's the transmission. brent ~$80 is the tactical level where short-covering extends, but the real signal is hormuz uncertainty leaking into diesel, propane, shipping and reserves — inflation pass-through that survives even if front-month crude stalls. own the complex where the physical market stays tight. $USO $CRAK

also on the desk: $NBIS washed-out sub-$170 but the bar is now contracted utilization, $META's $10b canada dc arguing against a capex stop, $OUST physical-ai perception via nvidia jetson, $HOOD chain optionality (early, distribution call not revenue).

bottom line: the tape is moving from "ai trade" to "ai underwriting." it pays receipts and punishes stories — $AVGO and $WYFI have them, $PENG had them last month, memory has them for 2028 but not for this week.

text: Nvidia $NVDA CEO Jensen Huang has announced a litany of partnerships over the past year focused on data centers, photonics technology, and more. I compiled a list of those partnerships and why each matters. https://t.co/D2NBUD74Uj

The Big Five are spending on AI faster than they're earning: @Microsoft, @Google, @amazon, @Meta. If CapEx exceeds cash flow, it can only persist as long as investors keep funding it. @PeterDiamandis asked me: What happens if sentiment shifts?

Here's my take: sentiment won’t shift. If anything, the investment will become more justified over time.

The world still sees compute as a rapidly depreciating asset but that’s simply not what it is anymore. Short of a massive leap in photonics or quantum computing, GPUs will continue to be highly sought after and have a predictable service life. This is already turning GPUs hours into a commodity.

Think about it like buying a house: when you buy a house, you finance it, and the house is collateral. In a world where compute becomes acceptable collateral, the hyperscalers could easily raise 10x to 100x on their cash flow in equity and debt, if they choose to.

In reality, there’s a lot of investable dollars in the world that want to flow into AI but can’t. AI is still the largest wealth creation moment in the history of humankind.

text:
Spot on. $SIVE is textbook.

Retail got positioned early on the AI photonics thesis, institutions finally show up (JP Morgan 5%+), then boom, dilution shakeout + noise drops it 65% from the highs while the pipeline and partnerships keep firing.

Swedish sellers out, US buyers (and big money) stepping in on the cheap. Classic transfer.
The narrative isn’t dead, it’s just getting loaded quietly. AI data center optics demand isn’t pausing.
Backing up the truck on these dips.

text:
Institutional Lenders Just Swapped $12M in Debt for $SIVE Equity at the Bottom. If You’re Selling $SIVE Here, You’re Trading Against the Smart Money.

Until a month ago, everyone was bullish on $SIVE at the top. Now everyone is max bearish because it’s down 55% from ATH. Classic retail psychology. People see red, panic, and completely miss the structural reality of what’s actually happening beneath the surface for $SIVE.

If you are short $SIVE here, you're basically betting the entire AI data center physical layout is going to grind to a halt. But look at how the puzzle pieces are locking together right now for $SIVE:

The Silicon Valley Pure-Play Carve-Out: The crowd is totally blind to this value play. $SIVE is merging its hyper-growth photonics business with Nordic Acquisition to spin it off into a standalone, U.S. Nasdaq-listed entity. By liberating this unit into the heart of the tech ecosystem, $SIVE is putting its indispensable Indium Phosphide (InP) laser platform right in front of deep-pocketed American AI capital while keeping controlling ownership.

The Institutional Vote of Confidence: Skeptics keep crying about paper dilution, but they completely ignored what just crossed the wire. A top-tier institutional lender (Bootstrap Europe) chose to convert their entire $12M loan straight into ordinary $SIVE equity at these exact lower levels. Smart money didn't want their cash back; they took 22 million shares of equity instead because they know the optical supplier thesis is fully intact.

The Weaponized Balance Sheet: That recent SEK 700 million directed share issue wasn’t generic fundraising, it was a calculated move. Equipment lead times for high-purity cleanrooms are hovering at 9 to 12 months. $SIVE proactively secured the cash today to expand its InP manufacturing capacity. If you aren’t spending that CAPEX right now, you completely miss the 2027 demand curve.

The Reality Check: Near-term execution risks, corporate restructuring friction, and high beta mean localized volatility for $SIVE will stay extreme. Microcaps transitioning into multi-entity setups always face messy trailing quarters before peak commercial production volume ramps late next year.

But if you look past the standard retail panic, $SIVE just systematically wiped out its external debt, capitalized its capacity expansions, and established a direct pathway to Silicon Valley valuation multiples. This isn't a structural breakdown for $SIVE, it is a massive accumulation window.

Holding my core $SIVE positions without a single trim.

text:
— Kyber NVL144, the flagship 2027 rack, delayed 12+ months to 2028. The PCB midplane can’t be manufactured at scale
— The backup architecture (NVL72x2) — cancelled
— The 4-die Rubin Ultra was already dead: packaging physically warps at that size. The 2-die survivor delivers roughly half the performance per package
— Optical scale-up (CPO) won’t arrive until Feynman

This is not one product slipping. It’s two physical walls — packaging AND interconnect — hitting in the same cycle. The “new rack every year” promise is what’s breaking.

WINNERS

— $GOOGL / $AVGO — the only proven alternative scale-up architecture on earth (optical switching, running in production for years). The 2027 window belongs to the TPU ecosystem
— $AMD — gets a window. A window is not a win. Execution at rack scale is unproven
— Pluggable optics: $COHR, $FN, $AAOI — CPO was the sword hanging over pluggables. It just got sheathed for another generation. The recent photonics selloff shot the wrong victim
— Copper: $APH, $CRDO — copper stays in the rack longer than anyone modeled
— Neoclouds: $NBIS, $CRWV — slower chip cadence means GPU fleets obsolete slower. The depreciation treadmill every bear cites just lost a year

LOSERS

— $NVDA — not revenue. They’ll sell every mature-gen rack they can make. What’s damaged is the moat premium: NVLink supremacy and the annual cadence ARE the multiple
— CPO pure plays: $POET, Sivers — the entire thesis is co-packaged optics adoption timing. That timing just moved right by a full generation. The biggest losers (Ayar Labs, Celestial AI) are private — you can’t even trade them
— $LITE — the trap. CPO laser story delayed, pluggable laser business extended. Net wash. Not the short people will think it is

THE $MU QUESTION

Do the math before you touch the sell button. Old Kyber rack: 144 packages x 16 HBM stacks = 2,304. New 2+2 config: 288 packages x 8 stacks = 2,304. Bit demand per rack: identical. What died is the “HBM per GPU doubles forever” narrative — a multiple story, not a bits story. And a flatter performance curve means buyers need MORE units for the same compute, in a market that’s supply-constrained until 2028.

Neutral on fundamentals. If it does trade down on the headline, then that gap will be called opportunity.

The nuance everyone will miss: NVIDIA can still hit rack-level compute targets with 2+2. Parity — bought with twice the silicon. The spec sheet survives. Cost per FLOP doesn’t.

The only question for 2027: can anyone exploit the gap before Feynman closes it? Exactly one company has a working alternative today.

Not financial advice.

text:
Bullish $SIVE: AMD is building CPO for the MI500 (2027) through GlobalFoundries, bypassing NVIDIA’s TSMC packaging bottleneck entirely. Since June, Sivers’ laser arrays are integrated into GF’s silicon photonics reference designs and SCALE CPO platform. If AMD’s optical scale-up bet materializes at GF, Sivers lasers are in the supply chain. NVIDIA stumbling widens exactly the window AMD and its photonics partners need.

Bullish $AAOI: every year CPO slips at NVIDIA is another year pluggable transceivers remain the workhorse of AI datacenters. Delays don’t destroy optical demand, they redirect capital back to pluggables and NPO. That’s AAOI’s core business. The market dumped it 17% on the delay headline without asking who actually benefits from it.

One company wins if CPO accelerates outside NVIDIA. The other wins because it’s delayed inside NVIDIA. Both win from the same news.

Delays don’t kill demand. They redistribute it.

text:
Rosenblatt: Optical

Context Behind Recent Sector Weakness

> China InP Capacity Scare: Stocks traded poorly over the last 1–2 months due to fears that expanding indium phosphide (InP) capacity in China could crash laser pricing.

> CPO Timing Rumors: Reports suggested a potential one-year delay for large-scale Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) deployment compared to the base expectation of a 2H27 build and 2028 shipment.

> Short Seller Dynamics: Checks indicate short sellers are exploiting this mid-quarter noise without genuine conviction. Many plan to cover their positions before 2QCY26 earnings roll out in late July/early August and intend to go long on Optical stocks by 2027 to capture the 2028–2030 CPO ramp.

Assessing the China InP Threat

> Supply vs. Demand: Chinese players could add 20% to 40% to Rosenblatt's 2030 non-China InP laser capacity forecast of ~$26bn. However, total 2030 InP capacity is still projected to fall 40% behind aggregate demand, mitigating oversupply risks.

> Technological Moat: Near-term Western pricing remains secure because Chinese manufacturers currently lack 200G EMLs and high-power CW (continuous wave) lasers required for CPO and Near-Packaged Optics (NPO) applications. They currently possess low-power CWs for SiPo transceivers and 100G EMLs for 800G transceivers.

> Market Decoupling: Long-term Western and Chinese supply chain dynamics are unlikely to be fungible due to political decoupling and the technical shift toward CPO. Western leaders like Lumentum (LITE) and Coherent (COHR) are already fully sold out through 2027 and into 2028, bolstered by major pre-buys from companies like NVIDIA.

Dongshan Precision: Recently greenlit a $1.2bn expansion in Changzhou for InP lasers and 1.6T transceivers after acquiring Source Photonics.

Sanan IC: Pivoting from LEDs to InP, expanding capacity from 2,750 to 6,000 6-inch wafers per month.

HiSense Optoelectronics & Accelink: Also actively growing InP capacity

Architecture & Structural Industry Demands

> CPO Timeline Intact: The smaller-scale CPO timeline (2H26 build, 2027 ship) remains on track, with 2027 production estimates ranging from 30K to over 350K units each for NVIDIA and Broadcom. 2028 remains the definitive volume inflection year for large-scale CPO, with NPO filling the intermediate gap in 2027.

> Network Flattening Already Priced In: Concerns that technologies like Multipath Reliable Connection (MRC) and Resilient Network Graphs (RNG) will hurt demand are overblown. While larger radix Ethernet switches reduce transceivers per switch from 6–7 down to 3–4, exponential XPU volume growth easily offsets the lower ratio.

> Alternative Laser Architectures (GaAs / VCSELs): 200G VCSELs (made from Gallium Arsenide rather than InP) are emerging as intermediate NPO solutions. Broadcom is looking at them for 3.2T NPO platforms, while Lumentum and startup PicoJool have demonstrated VCSEL-based solutions to circumvent potential InP supply constraints in GPU-to-GPU interconnects.

Key Company Earnings Setups

> Lumentum ($LITE): Toughest near-term setup because it has provided the most forward guidance, though it remains highly attractive at ~$700. Investors are watching if it can accelerate its CY27 target of $2bn in quarterly revenue with 40+% operating margins.

> Coherent ($COHR): Highly favored due to accelerating internal 200G EML/CW laser production and a major new ~$1bn/year 1.6T transceiver contract win with Google (sourcing Mitsubishi lasers). Long-term margins will be supported by 11-figure LTAs with NVIDIA for 2027–2030.

> Applied Optoelectronics ($AAOI): Expected to deliver a "beat and raise" watershed 2Q26. Driven by 800G sales to Amazon and Oracle, revenues are expected to exceed $200mn in 2Q26, with 3Q26 guidance pointing to ~$300mn and gross margin expansion well north of the ~30% level seen in 1H26.

> Arista Networks ($ANET): Positioned for strong growth via massive deployment wins with Meta, Microsoft, Oracle, OpenAI/Anthropic, and Google (which could become a 10% customer in 2026). High deferred revenue recognition is expected to fuel >30% revenue growth in 2H26 and 40+% in 2027.

> Fabrinet ($FN): Positioned for solid sequential Datacom growth with NVIDIA alongside initial revenue contributions from new Datacom customers Amazon and Lumentum.

> Viavi Solutions ($VIAV): Safe from InP noise as it has no direct exposure. Growth is driven by testing systems (transceiver/OCS) and MilAero products, with CPO/NPO testing revenues slated to begin this fall.

> Ciena ($CIEN): Ciena's order book suggest that Data Center Interconnect (DCI) and scale-out orders are poised to reaccelerate from previous levels.

text:
$AEHR closing 72.69 with VWAP near 72.64 and still 24% under the 50 day at 95.19 says you are mid corrective wave after a monster +400% year, not at the start of the next moonshot, even with the new silicon photonics order.

BUY dips into 65 to 70 only if you can stomach 20% swings, and aim to TRIM around 90 to 100 where the 50 day and prior breakdown zone sit, because volatility here will throw weak hands out before any new high ever prints.
@jiahanjimliu @CockedStriker @SemiAnalysis_ The photonics fud on AAOI from semi analysis made me lose trust with Nvidia.

text:
AI Bottlenecks Explained:

Key Current Bottlenecks
•Power/Energy — Primary constraint: Massive electricity demand for compute + cooling; grid delays, transformers, and generation shortages.

        •Memory (HBM) — High-bandwidth memory supply sold out; critical for GPU performance.

         •Networking/Interconnects — Data movement between GPUs (optical, switches, photonics) causing bottlenecks.

         •Cooling & Power Delivery — Liquid cooling and electrical systems strained by high-density AI racks.

Stocks Benefiting Most:

Power/Energy (hottest area):
•Vertiv $VRT — Cooling & power management.
•GE Vernova  $GEV — Turbines for new power plants.
•Bloom Energy  $BE — On-site fuel cells.
•Constellation Energy  $CEG — Nuclear power restarts.
Memory/Semiconductors:
•Micron $MU — HBM leader.
•TSMC $TSM — Chip manufacturing/packaging.
•Broadcom $AVGO — Networking & custom chips.
Networking & Cooling:
•Arista $ANET, Coherent $COHR. — Optics & switches.
•Vertiv/Modine — Liquid cooling.

Outlook: Hyperscalers spending $700B+ on AI infrastructure in 2026. 
Power/infra plays gaining as GPU supply eases. Volatile theme—focus on execution and backlogs.

Not financial advice.

text:
$IREN: SemiAnalysis Hit Piece In The Works

@SemiAnalysis_ is a great research shop except for when they write the occasional hit piece like recently on  Photonics and Nvidia.

I'm hearing a rumor that @SemiAnalysis_ is preparing a hit piece on $IREN's Childress site while it was ramping.

They have old satellite images that show H1 was using chiller trucks. These were temporary and H1 is now on permanent stationary chillers. 

I'm not sure what else they have in store but if the hit piece  ever launches, know that it will be dispelled when IREN announces GB300 exemplar status and handover H1 to $MSFT. 

Coincidentally, this algins with rising short interest from 15% to 22.51% since May (1). This is before the GSW Marketing and RSU compensation package so it's a short prepared in advance of those two events and will likely top of with a SemiAnalysis Piece.

H1 handover late July. Hit piece will not matter afterwards.

✨Be included in OPN's "Optics in 2026" issue✨ 

Have you conducted notable peer-reviewed research in the optics and photonics field? Send us a brief summary to be featured in the year-end special! 

More information via the link: https://t.co/sIvClvYHcG 

#Optics #Photonics https://t.co/hV6yct2zpH

> looking into packaging, possibly $AMKR 

> no energy/space/software, for now, otherwise too much diversification

text:
Mirae Asset Securities: AI Bottleneck Issue

Power Bottleneck

> The Moment for Analog Semiconductors: The value of the analog domain, where Moore's Law does not apply, is exploding, giving suppliers overwhelming pricing power. Major players like TI, NXP, and Infineon are proving this dominant supplier market by executing synchronized price hikes in 2026.

> The Physics of 12,500 Amperes and 800V DC: In ultra-high-density AI racks of 600kW to 1MW, sticking to traditional 48V power distribution causes the current to skyrocket to 12,500A, making operations impossible due to heat loss. Therefore, shifting the architecture to 800V to reduce the current to 750A and cut copper requirements by 45% is a physical inevitability.

> Vertical Power Delivery (VPD): To prevent loss and voltage fluctuations during the "last inch" of stepping down to the GPU core voltage (around 0.7V), VPD—essentially the "CPO of the power world"—is emerging, which places the power converter directly beneath the GPU die.

Signal & Photonics Bottleneck

> Distance-Based Redistribution of Copper and Optics: The simplistic narrative that "optics will completely wipe out copper" is incorrect. A distance-based redistribution is intensifying, where copper and analog handle short distances (the scale-up domain) and optics take over long distances (rack-to-rack connectivity).

> Semtech’s Analog Magic: In 200G/lane high-speed communication, Semtech's technology is garnering massive attention by completely eliminating DSPs (Digital Signal Processors)—which consume heavy power and cause latency—and correcting signals using only Analog Equalizers. This reduces power consumption by tenfold and slashes latency from nanoseconds (ns) to picoseconds (ps).

> The Real Bottleneck in Optical Tech is the 'Light Source': While Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) is the correct long-term direction, the realistic winners for the next 2 to 3 years are Near-Packaged Optics (NPO) and 1.6T pluggable modules. The true bottlenecks in optical communication lie below the finished optical module layer: InP (Indium Phosphide) substrates, Continuous Wave (CW) laser sources, Photonics-SOI wafers (monopolized by Soitec), and SiPho foundries.

> Network Reliability and 'Link Flap': The "Link Flap" phenomenon, where an optical link momentarily blinks, disrupts the synchronization (all-reduce) of the entire AI cluster and leaves GPUs idling. In massive clusters, solutions that guarantee reliability rather than just bandwidth (such as Credo's Zero-Flap) become the ultimate bottleneck determining economic viability.

"richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 1798}, {"from_index": 2205, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 2241}]}, "text": "Mirae Asset Securities: AI Bottleneck Issue Power Bottleneck > The Moment for Analog Semiconductors: The value of the analog domain, where Moore's Law does not apply, is exploding, giving suppliers overwhelming pricing power. Major players like TI, NXP, and Infineon are proving this dominant supplier market by executing synchronized price hikes in 2026. > The Physics of 12,500 Amperes and 800V DC: In ultra-high-density AI racks of 600kW to 1MW, sticking to traditional 48V power distribution causes the current to skyrocket to 12,500A, making operations impossible due to heat loss. Therefore, shifting the architecture to 800V to reduce the current to 750A and cut copper requirements by 45% is a physical inevitability. > Vertical Power Delivery (VPD): To prevent loss and voltage fluctuations during the "last inch" of stepping down to the GPU core voltage (around 0.7V), VPD—essentially the "CPO of the power world"—is emerging, which places the power converter directly beneath the GPU die. Signal & Photonics Bottleneck > Distance-Based Redistribution of Copper and Optics: The simplistic narrative that "optics will completely wipe out copper" is incorrect. A distance-based redistribution is intensifying, where copper and analog handle short distances (the scale-up domain) and optics take over long distances (rack-to-rack connectivity). > Semtech’s Analog Magic: In 200G/lane high-speed communication, Semtech's technology is garnering massive attention by completely eliminating DSPs (Digital Signal Processors)—which consume heavy power and cause latency—and correcting signals using only Analog Equalizers. This reduces power consumption by tenfold and slashes latency from nanoseconds (ns) to picoseconds (ps). > The Real Bottleneck in Optical Tech is the 'Light Source': While Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) is the correct long-term direction, the realistic winners for the next 2 to 3 years are Near-Packaged Optics (NPO) and 1.6T pluggable modules. The true bottlenecks in optical communication lie below the finished optical module layer: InP (Indium Phosphide) substrates, Continuous Wave (CW) laser sources, Photonics-SOI wafers (monopolized by Soitec), and SiPho foundries. > Network Reliability and 'Link Flap': The "Link Flap" phenomenon, where an optical link momentarily blinks, disrupts the synchronization (all-reduce) of the entire AI cluster and leaves GPUs idling. In massive clusters, solutions that guarantee reliability rather than just bandwidth (such as Credo's Zero-Flap) become the ultimate bottleneck determining economic viability. $TSEM $COHR $LITE $AAOI $MRVL $LITE $TSM $INTC $GFS $FN $SMTC $MTSI $APH $CRDO"}}}, "rest_id": "2075693388158894328", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "45649", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

user_name: My name is M. Only M
text:
AVANCES EN TELEPORTACIÓN CUÁNTICA: EL CAMINO HACIA UN INTERNET ULTRASEGURO

Investigadores de la Universidad de Ciencia y Tecnología de China han logrado un avance significativo en la teleportación cuántica a gran escala utilizando redes de fibra óptica de larga distancia, un paso crucial para el desarrollo de un internet cuántico prácticamente incorruptible.

El experimento aprovecha el entrelazamiento cuántico, el fenómeno que Albert Einstein denominó "acción fantasmal a la distancia", donde el estado de dos partículas vinculadas se correlaciona sin importar la separación física entre ellas. Este sistema permite transferir estados cuánticos (información de la partícula) entre nodos distantes sin necesidad de desplazar la materia física en sí.

Sin embargo, es importante aclarar un mito común en la física: este proceso no permite la comunicación instantánea ni supera la velocidad de la luz. Para que la teleportación cuántica se complete con éxito, los científicos siempre necesitan enviar datos adicionales a través de canales tradicionales (como cables de fibra óptica convencionales), lo que limita la velocidad final de la transmisión a los límites impuestos por la teoría de la relatividad.

El verdadero hito de este avance no radica en la velocidad, sino en la seguridad y la fidelidad de la red. Al entrelazar las partículas, cualquier intento de interceptar, medir o espiar la información destruye de inmediato el estado cuántico, creando un ecosistema criptográfico inmune a los ciberataques y garantizando una privacidad digital absoluta para el futuro.

Fuente: University of Science and Technology of China | Nature Photonics | MIT Technology Review

[]}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzU2ODkyNTU1MDI4MzU3MTI=", "text": "AVANCES EN TELEPORTACIÓN CUÁNTICA: EL CAMINO HACIA UN INTERNET ULTRASEGURO Investigadores de la Universidad de Ciencia y Tecnología de China han logrado un avance significativo en la teleportación cuántica a gran escala utilizando redes de fibra óptica de larga distancia, un paso crucial para el desarrollo de un internet cuántico prácticamente incorruptible. El experimento aprovecha el entrelazamiento cuántico, el fenómeno que Albert Einstein denominó "acción fantasmal a la distancia", donde el estado de dos partículas vinculadas se correlaciona sin importar la separación física entre ellas. Este sistema permite transferir estados cuánticos (información de la partícula) entre nodos distantes sin necesidad de desplazar la materia física en sí. Sin embargo, es importante aclarar un mito común en la física: este proceso no permite la comunicación instantánea ni supera la velocidad de la luz. Para que la teleportación cuántica se complete con éxito, los científicos siempre necesitan enviar datos adicionales a través de canales tradicionales (como cables de fibra óptica convencionales), lo que limita la velocidad final de la transmisión a los límites impuestos por la teoría de la relatividad. El verdadero hito de este avance no radica en la velocidad, sino en la seguridad y la fidelidad de la red. Al entrelazar las partículas, cualquier intento de interceptar, medir o espiar la información destruye de inmediato el estado cuántico, creando un ecosistema criptográfico inmune a los ciberataques y garantizando una privacidad digital absoluta para el futuro. text:
Quantum Technology: A New Kind of Technology

Quantum technology uses the strange behavior of extremely small particles to create powerful new tools.

Scientists are developing quantum computers, communication systems, and sensors that may someday improve medicine, cybersecurity, navigation, energy, and scientific research.

What Is a Quantum Computer?

Ordinary computers use bits that represent either a zero or a one.

Quantum computers use quantum bits, called qubits. Qubits can behave in unusual ways that may allow a quantum computer to examine certain difficult problems much faster than an ordinary computer.

This does not mean quantum computers will replace phones, laptops, or regular computers. They are being designed for special problems that are extremely difficult to solve.

How Could Quantum Computers Help?

Quantum computers may eventually help scientists:

Discover new medicines
Design stronger materials
Improve batteries
Study complicated molecules
Plan transportation routes
Develop cleaner energy
Understand diseases
Improve weather and climate research

Many of these possibilities are still being tested. Quantum computers are not yet powerful or reliable enough to perform most of these tasks better than ordinary computers.

Why Are Quantum Computers So Difficult to Build?

Qubits are extremely fragile.

Heat, vibration, electrical interference, and tiny manufacturing errors can disturb them. Some quantum computers must be kept at temperatures colder than outer space.

Scientists are working to reduce errors and keep quantum information stable long enough to complete useful calculations.

Quantum Sensors

Quantum sensors may become useful sooner than large quantum computers.

These sensors can make extremely precise measurements. They may help doctors see inside the body, help ships or aircraft navigate when GPS is unavailable, locate objects underground, monitor the environment, and improve scientific research.

Atomic clocks are an example of quantum technology already being used today. They help support GPS, communications, banking, and other important systems.

Quantum Communication

Quantum communication may provide new ways to protect sensitive information.

It can use the laws of quantum physics to reveal whether someone has attempted to intercept a message.

However, no communication system is completely secure. Computers, software, passwords, and human mistakes can still create weaknesses.

Could Quantum Computers Break Encryption?

A powerful future quantum computer may be able to break some of the encryption currently protecting websites, financial systems, government information, and private communications.

Present quantum computers cannot do this on a large scale.

Governments and technology companies are already developing new forms of encryption designed to withstand future quantum attacks. This is called post-quantum cryptography.

Is Quantum Technology Being Exaggerated?

There is genuine progress, although there is also a great deal of publicity and exaggeration.

Companies sometimes announce impressive numbers of qubits without clearly explaining how reliable those qubits are. A machine with many unstable qubits may be less useful than a smaller and more accurate machine.

Promises about instant medical cures, unbeatable artificial intelligence, or computers that solve every problem should be treated carefully.

Why Does This Matter?

Quantum technology could eventually improve medicine, strengthen cybersecurity, create better materials, support cleaner energy, and provide more accurate measuring tools.

It could also increase military competition, threaten older encryption, and give powerful countries or corporations greater technological control.

Quantum technology is not magic, and it is not ready to transform everyday life immediately. It is an important developing field that may gradually change science, security, medicine, and industry.

The wisest response is neither fear nor blind excitement. We should follow its progress carefully, prepare for its risks, and use its discoveries responsibly.

Quantum technology seeks to control some of the smallest and strangest features of nature and turn them into useful machines.

The field includes far more than futuristic computers. It also includes extraordinarily sensitive measuring instruments, highly accurate clocks, secure communication methods, and networks that may someday connect quantum devices across great distances.

The three most developed areas are quantum computing, quantum communication, and quantum sensing. Each uses the laws of quantum mechanics differently, and each is progressing at a different speed.

What Makes Something Quantum?

Ordinary computers store and process information using bits. A bit represents either a zero or a one.

A quantum computer uses quantum bits, usually called qubits. Under controlled conditions, a qubit can exist in a combination of possible states known as superposition. Multiple qubits can also become entangled, meaning their properties are connected in ways that cannot be fully explained by treating each particle separately.

Quantum algorithms manipulate these combined possibilities through interference. Some possible answers are strengthened, while others are weakened or canceled. When the qubits are measured, however, the delicate quantum state collapses into an ordinary result.

This does not mean that a quantum computer simply tries every possible answer at once and instantly selects the correct one. That popular explanation is too simplistic. A useful quantum algorithm must be carefully designed so that interference makes the desired information more likely to appear when the system is measured.

The Problem of Quantum Fragility

Quantum states are extremely delicate.

Heat, vibration, electromagnetic interference, imperfect controls, microscopic manufacturing defects, and interactions with the surrounding environment can introduce errors. The loss of usable quantum behavior is known as decoherence.

Some quantum computers therefore operate inside elaborate refrigeration systems at temperatures close to absolute zero. Other designs use trapped ions, neutral atoms, photons, semiconductor devices, or other physical systems.

Each approach has advantages and weaknesses. Researchers have not yet established one universally superior form of quantum hardware.

The number of qubits also tells only part of the story. A machine containing many unreliable qubits may accomplish less than one containing fewer, higher-quality qubits. Important measurements include error rates, connectivity, operating speed, coherence time, and the complexity of circuits the machine can execute accurately.

Quantum Error Correction

A fully useful quantum computer will probably require quantum error correction.

Because unknown quantum information cannot simply be copied like an ordinary computer file, error correction must distribute information across groups of physical qubits. Together, these physical qubits form a more dependable logical qubit.

This process requires substantial hardware. Depending on the quality of the components and the error-correction method, one reliable logical qubit may require many physical qubits.

Progress in error correction is therefore often more meaningful than announcements based only on total qubit counts. The central challenge is not merely constructing a larger processor. It is making the entire system more reliable as it grows.

Quantum Computing

Quantum computers are specialized machines intended to perform certain calculations using quantum effects.

They are not expected to replace laptops, phones, conventional servers, or supercomputers. Most daily tasks—including word processing, video playback, email, accounting, web browsing, and database management—are handled more efficiently by ordinary computers.

The more realistic future is hybrid computing. Conventional processors will manage most of a workload, while a quantum processor may be assigned a narrow calculation for which it offers a genuine advantage.

IBM, for example, has published plans for closer integration between quantum systems and classical supercomputers, along with tools for monitoring and debugging hybrid workloads. These are company roadmaps rather than guarantees of when broad commercial advantage will arrive.

Promising Uses of Quantum Computers

Chemistry and medicine

Nature is quantum mechanical at the molecular level. Simulating molecules accurately becomes extremely difficult for classical computers as their complexity increases.

Future quantum computers may help scientists model chemical reactions, proteins, catalysts, and pharmaceutical compounds. This could support drug discovery, although quantum computers would not eliminate laboratory testing, clinical trials, safety reviews, or the biological complexity of disease.

Materials and energy

Quantum simulation may help researchers investigate improved batteries, superconductors, fertilizers, solar materials, industrial catalysts, and methods of capturing carbon.

Even modest improvements in catalysts could reduce the energy required by major industrial processes. The difficulty is reaching a scale and accuracy that outperform the best classical methods on genuinely valuable problems.

Optimization

Organizations face complicated scheduling, routing, manufacturing, investment, and supply-chain problems.

Quantum algorithms may eventually help with certain forms of optimization. However, many optimization claims remain experimental. Classical algorithms also continue to improve, so a quantum method must be compared with the strongest conventional alternative rather than an outdated one.

Scientific discovery

Quantum computers may become valuable research instruments for nuclear physics, high-energy physics, materials science, and other fields involving complex quantum systems.

The Department of Energy has identified quantum information science as relevant to materials research, environmental measurement, navigation, dark-matter searches, gravitational-wave detection, and other scientific challenges.

Artificial intelligence

Quantum computing and artificial intelligence may eventually complement one another. AI can help calibrate quantum devices, analyze experimental results, and search for improved control methods.

Quantum algorithms might someday accelerate selected machine-learning calculations. Claims that quantum computers will automatically make all AI dramatically more powerful remain unproven.

Quantum Communication

Quantum communication transfers or shares information using quantum states, often carried by photons.

One important concept is quantum key distribution. It can allow two parties to create encryption keys while detecting certain attempts to intercept the quantum transmission. Measurement disturbs quantum states, so eavesdropping can leave detectable evidence.

Quantum key distribution does not make an entire communication system invulnerable. Hardware, software, users, authentication systems, and network endpoints can still be attacked.

Researchers are also working toward quantum networks that could connect sensors, processors, memories, and communication devices. Entanglement might eventually be distributed across these networks, enabling capabilities that ordinary networks cannot reproduce. NIST is developing components, prototypes, and test beds for quantum networking.

A large-scale “quantum internet” remains a long-term research goal. It would not simply be a faster version of today’s internet. It would likely operate alongside classical networks and provide specialized functions.

Quantum Sensing

Quantum sensing may affect society sooner than large fault-tolerant quantum computing.

Quantum sensors use properties such as atomic energy levels, spin, interference, and entanglement to measure time, gravity, magnetic fields, acceleration, rotation, temperature, and other physical quantities with exceptional precision.

Some quantum-based instruments already exist. Atomic clocks support precise timekeeping, navigation, telecommunications, scientific measurement, and the operation of modern infrastructure.

Emerging quantum sensors may help locate underground structures, detect subtle magnetic signals, improve medical imaging, monitor environmental changes, measure movement without relying entirely on GPS, and conduct fundamental scientific experiments.

Quantum sensing often does not require the enormous error-corrected processors needed for universal quantum computing. This may allow useful sensing products to mature earlier.

Navigation Without GPS

Modern transportation, communications, finance, energy systems, and military operations depend heavily upon accurate timing and satellite navigation.

Quantum accelerometers, gyroscopes, clocks, and gravimeters could contribute to navigation systems that continue operating when GPS signals are blocked, jammed, unavailable, or unreliable.

Such systems would not necessarily make satellites unnecessary. They could provide an additional layer of resilience for aircraft, ships, emergency services, research vessels, infrastructure, and national defense.

The Encryption Challenge

A sufficiently powerful fault-tolerant quantum computer could threaten widely used public-key cryptographic systems.

These systems help protect websites, financial transactions, government communications, software updates, digital identities, and private records. The danger does not mean present quantum machines can suddenly break all modern encryption. They cannot.

The concern is partly long-term. An attacker could collect encrypted information today and retain it until future technology becomes capable of decrypting it. This is sometimes described as “harvest now, decrypt later.”

The principal defense is post-quantum cryptography: conventional mathematical encryption designed to resist attacks from both classical and quantum computers.

Moving to new cryptographic systems takes years because organizations must identify vulnerable systems, update software and hardware, test compatibility, replace long-lived equipment, and protect archived information. Preparation should therefore begin before a cryptographically dangerous quantum computer exists.

Quantum communication and post-quantum cryptography should not be confused. One uses quantum physical systems; the other generally runs on conventional computers.

Where the Field Stands in 2026

Quantum research has entered a period of large public and private investment.

In November 2025, the U.S. Department of Energy announced $625 million to renew five National Quantum Information Science Research Centers. IBM announced in June 2026 that it planned to invest more than $10 billion in quantum computing over five years. These commitments show strategic confidence in the field, although investment does not guarantee that every technical roadmap will be achieved on schedule.

Researchers are improving control systems, fabrication, modular architectures, networking, software, and error-correction techniques. Companies are also attempting to integrate quantum processors with classical high-performance computing.

At the same time, the field remains vulnerable to exaggerated announcements. Experimental demonstrations may solve artificial benchmark problems without producing immediate commercial value. Company claims can also face scientific dispute, as recent criticism of Microsoft’s topological-qubit research illustrates.

Responsible evaluation requires published evidence, independent replication, meaningful benchmarks, and comparison with the best available classical machines.

Major Obstacles

Noise and errors

Qubits lose information and accumulate errors rapidly. Useful computation may require far greater reliability than most present systems provide.

Scaling

A laboratory device containing a small number of controllable qubits is very different from an industrial system containing enough logical qubits for valuable fault-tolerant computation.

Manufacturing

Quantum hardware requires extraordinary precision. Tiny variations can affect the behavior of individual qubits and reduce production yield.

Cooling and infrastructure

Some architectures require complex cryogenic equipment, shielding, lasers, vacuum systems, control electronics, or specialized facilities.

Software

Quantum programming demands new algorithms, compilers, verification methods, and ways to divide work between quantum and classical processors.

Verification

A difficult question arises when a quantum computer produces a result that no classical computer can independently reproduce. Researchers need trustworthy methods to verify the calculation.

Cost

Quantum systems remain expensive to build, operate, maintain, and calibrate. Early access will probably be concentrated in governments, universities, cloud providers, and large companies.

Economic and Social Effects

Quantum technology may create demand for physicists, engineers, programmers, technicians, mathematicians, cybersecurity specialists, materials scientists, and manufacturing workers.

The field also requires people who can connect scientific research with medicine, chemistry, business, law, ethics, and public policy.

Not every worker will need a doctorate in quantum physics. A mature industry will also require conventional software development, electronics, refrigeration, photonics, fabrication, maintenance, education, sales, and regulatory expertise.

At the same time, unequal access could concentrate scientific and economic power among a small number of countries and companies. Public research, education, shared infrastructure, international standards, and responsible cloud access may help broaden participation.

Military and Geopolitical Concerns

Quantum technology has implications for secure communication, navigation, surveillance, sensing, codebreaking, submarine detection, and advanced materials.

This makes it strategically important to governments. It also increases the danger of secrecy, international rivalry, export restrictions, and an expensive technological arms race.

Quantum research has many peaceful uses, yet the same capability may have both civilian and military applications. Nations will need to protect legitimate security interests while maintaining enough scientific cooperation to support safety, verification, and responsible standards.

Ethical Questions

Quantum technology raises several important questions:

Who should control the most powerful systems?

How should governments prevent misuse without blocking beneficial research?

How should claims of “quantum advantage” be tested?

How can society avoid wasting public money on hype?

How should privacy be protected during the transition to quantum-resistant security?

How can smaller nations, universities, and businesses participate without becoming permanently dependent on a few providers?

These questions should be addressed while the technology is developing, rather than after its effects become difficult to reverse.

What Quantum Technology Will Not Do

Quantum technology will not make every computer program faster.

It will not instantly cure disease, predict the future, eliminate uncertainty, make artificial intelligence all-knowing, or solve every mathematical problem.

Quantum entanglement does not permit ordinary information to travel faster than light.

Quantum computers also do not test every possibility and effortlessly reveal the correct answer. Their advantages depend upon particular algorithms, suitable hardware, low error rates, and problems whose structure can be exploited quantum mechanically.

Any claim that ignores these limitations should be treated cautiously.

A Realistic Outlook

Quantum sensing and timing are likely to deliver practical benefits earlier and more widely than universal fault-tolerant quantum computing.

Quantum communication will probably develop through specialized networks, government systems, research links, and security-sensitive applications before becoming broadly available.

Quantum computing may progress through increasingly capable hybrid systems. The decisive milestone will not be a dramatic qubit announcement. It will be the repeated solution of useful problems more accurately, quickly, or economically than the best classical alternatives.

That transition may be gradual. Classical computers will also continue advancing, creating a moving target that quantum systems must surpass.

Why Quantum Technology Matters to Ordinary People

Most people may never own a quantum computer, just as most people do not own a supercomputer or an atomic clock.

They may still benefit from the technology indirectly.

Quantum tools could contribute to safer medicines, stronger materials, improved batteries, more accurate medical instruments, resilient navigation, earlier detection of underground hazards, better scientific models, and stronger digital security.

They could also create risks if governments and businesses fail to modernize encryption, test claims carefully, control military applications responsibly, or ensure fair access.

Quantum technology should therefore be approached with neither blind excitement nor automatic fear. It is a serious developing field with genuine achievements, extraordinary possibilities, unresolved scientific barriers, and a large amount of uncertainty.

Its eventual influence will depend not only upon what researchers are able to build, but also upon the wisdom with which society chooses to use it.
